key_insight: The paper argues that autoformalization should be judged by semantic faithfulness, not just typechecking or provability, and proposes bidirectional proof-based fingerprints to detect meaning drift.
engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, BPF adds a practical verification layer that can filter unsafe formalizations and improve downstream autoformalization quality.
method: Bidirectional Provability Fingerprinting compares forward and backward consequence neighborhoods in the ambient theory.
Counterfactual Probe Generation creates contrastive probes to target specific drift directions.
Adaptive Probe Budget Allocation and Faithfulness-Guided Decoding use probe signals to spend verification effort efficiently and steer generation.
evidence_signal: On DriftBench (2,183 NL/Lean 4 pairs), BPF+cpg detects 89.6% of drifted formalizations at a 3.0% false-positive rate, outperforming typecheck and LLM-judge baselines.
watch_item: The approach depends on generating informative probes and theory-specific consequence checks, so scalability and coverage may vary with the complexity of the formal library.
paper_title: The Faithfulness Gap: Certifying Semantic Equivalence Between Natural-Language and Formal Mathematical Statements
